title: Inference Engine with Latency & Error Isolation
goal: Implement the core inference module that sends a prompt to a specified deployment, captures the response text and token usage, measures round-trip latency, and isolates failures so that one deployment's error does not affect others.

in_scope: Create a function to send a single prompt to a single deployment via the Azure AI Inference SDK
Capture and return: response text, input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens
Record start and end timestamps to compute latency in milliseconds
Catch all exceptions during the inference call and return a structured error result instead of raising
Create a batch execution function that calls multiple deployments sequentially and collects all results
Handle missing token usage metadata gracefully (return None for missing fields)
out_of_scope: Cost calculation (delegated to Part 04 pricing module)

inputs: PRD section 4 — Prompt input and execution, Latency measurement, Failure isolation
PRD user stories GH-004, GH-007, GH-008
outputs: src/inference.py — module with run_inference() and run_batch_inference() functions
Structured result dict per deployment: {deployment_name, model_name, output_text, input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens, latency_ms, error}
checklist: Create src/inference.py with a run_inference(client, deployment_name, model_type, prompt) function
Record time.perf_counter() before the inference call as start_time
Use azure.ai.inference ChatCompletionsClient (Azure AI Inference SDK) to send the prompt for chat/completions models
Record time.perf_counter() after the response as end_time
Calculate latency_ms = (end_time - start_time) * 1000, rounded to nearest integer
Extract response text from the SDK response object
Extract token usage: input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens from response.usage (if available)
If token usage is not available in the response, set token fields to None
Wrap the entire inference call in try/except Exception to catch all failures
On failure, return a result dict with error=str(exception) and all other fields set to None (except deployment_name)
On success, return a result dict with error=None and all fields populated
Create run_batch_inference(client, deployments, prompt) that iterates over deployments and calls run_inference for each
run_batch_inference must never raise — every deployment produces a result dict (success or error)
acceptance_criteria: run_inference() returns a dict with keys: deployment_name, model_name, output_text, input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens, latency_ms, error
On successful call, error is None and output_text contains the model response
On failed call, error contains a descriptive message and output_text is None
Latency is measured in milliseconds and reflects actual round-trip time
run_batch_inference() returns a list with one result dict per deployment, regardless of individual failures
A failure in deployment A does not prevent deployment B from executing
Token fields are None when the SDK response lacks usage metadata (no crash)
review_focus: Error isolation is robust — no exception propagation from individual inference calls
Latency measurement uses perf_counter (not datetime) for precision
The correct SDK client method is used for chat vs. completion model types
The prompt is sent as a proper message structure (e.g., [{role: 'user', content: prompt}] for chat models)
Result dict structure is consistent between success and error cases
risks_or_notes: Ensure ChatCompletionsClient is initialized with the deployment inference endpoint and AzureKeyCredential per the installed Azure AI Inference SDK version
Some deployments may have rate limits — sequential execution with no delay may trigger 429 errors
Response object structure may vary between model types — handle polymorphic responses
Long-running inference calls may benefit from a timeout parameter (consider adding a default timeout of 60 seconds)
The prompt structure (messages array vs. plain string) depends on whether the model is chat or completion type
definition_of_done: src/inference.py exists with run_inference() and run_batch_inference() functions
Success cases return complete result dicts with output text, tokens, and latency
Failure cases return error result dicts without raising exceptions
Batch execution isolates failures — each deployment gets its own result
Functions are testable with mocked SDK client
No global state mutation or side effects beyond the SDK call
